content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies that made you laugh so hard you cried

**Score:** 155
**Body:** I feel like I've exhausted the extent of my own repertoire/reserve of funny movies. I'd like to give something new a chance, so here I am, hoping you can help.

To give you an example of what I find funny, the new Naked Gun had me in tears at times (and I love the originals). The Office (US) is my favorite TV show of all time, so movies in that vein would work, too.

Edited to add: Y'all are amazing, thank you so much. I'm really enjoying the suggestions, especially your anecdotes around them. I'm getting some good ones! Keep it up!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Airplane, surely
  - Don't call me Shirley!
2. The birdcage
  - It’s da choes. They make me fall down.
3. Tropic Thunder
4. 40 yr old virgin
5. The Hangover....but ONLY if you haven't seen it. I was still laughing 2 days later.
6. There's Something About Mary
7. Young Frankenstein.  Mr Mom.   Let it Ride.   
8. None. But have always loved Rat Race

## 2. Any other non-linear storytelling movies like Memento?

**Score:** 50
**Body:** Looking for recs where the story does not unfold linerally, meaning there are time jumps, or flashbacks, or just overall a more disjointed story telling. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
2. Go (1999)

Pulp Fiction (1994)

21 Grams (2003)

Irreversible (2002)
  - Never seen Go. Not sure how I’ve missed that one, will check it out
    - Ahh he beat me to it but hell *yes*, Go is awesome.
3. If you want one that will break your brain and it not only involves time jumps but time travel, check out Predestination.
4. One Cut of the Dead

Weapons

The Haunting of Hill House (Miniseries)
5. Pulp Fiction
6. Mulholland Drive

Gone Girl 

Black Swan
7. The Red Violin (1998) is a masterpiece. Forgotten about gem.
8. Arrival  
Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind  
Run Lola Run (?)

## 3. Any good alien movies that arnt Alien?

**Score:** 37
**Body:** Im in the mood for an atmospheric alien movie that isn't the Alien movies lol. Something creepy and atmospheric, alao possibly one where the aliens win and/or are the good guys, but im not to bothered if not. I just want a hood alien movie that isn't all just alien action fighting if you get my mean

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Thing
2. Arrival (2016)

Skylines (2020)

Mickey 17 (2025)
  - \+1 for Arrival
3. No One Will Save You is great
4. Enemy Mine! 


In the far future, humanity is at war with an alien species, Dennis Quaid plays a fighter pilot who crash lands on an uninhabited world, realizes that one of the aliens (Louis Gusset Jr) he's at war with is stranded there as well, and the two have to learn to work together to survive. Really good acting from both, and the film takes a kind of weird turn (in a good way). Lots of really good alien culture stuff. 
5. The Vast of Night
6. Pandorum (2009). Kind of an underrated movie, but well worth a watch.
7. Invasion of the Body Snatchers (1978)

The Thing (1982)
8. Annihilation is excellent.
9. District 9

## 4. Looking for a mind-bending movie to watch

**Score:** 30
**Body:** I’m in the mood for something that’ll really make me think. Not just a regular thriller, but something with twists, deep themes, or a really unique storyline. Think Inception, Shutter Island, or The Prestige.

What’s the most mind-bending movie you’ve seen? Something that sticks with you long after the credits roll?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jacob’s Ladder
2. It's What's Inside, Coherence.
3. Mulholland Drive (2001)

Predestination (2014)

Enemy (2013)

12 Monkeys (1995)

Memento (2000)
4. Vanilla Sky.
5. Lost Highway
6. Triangle
7. The Handmaiden or Oldboy.
8. Altered States

Pi
9. Primer
10. Perfect Blue

## 5. movies that show women as winners/ where women are happy

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Hello, I have realised that lately I’ve been consuming films that contain a lot of women suffering. The state of the world is awful as is, so I would like something more positive. I am tired of seeing women suffer, I want them to win and be happy.  

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Romy and Michele's High School Reunion
2. Barbie

Erin Brockovich

Wild

The First Wives Club

Whale Rider

Everything Everywhere All At Once

Desperately Seeking Susan

Muriel's Wedding

Wonder Woman

The Fifth Element
3. Death Proof - Hilarious movie where the women definitely win in the end!
  - One of my absolute faves
4. Kiki’s Delivery Service

Princess Mononoke
5. Kill Bill
6. Bridesmaids is great.  Though Melissa McCarthy is definitely suffering in one of the scenes 😂
7. The Help 

Hidden Figures

She Said

Edit: These films include various trials and tribulations but they have your desired outcome!
8. Chicago! also Lilith Fair: Building a Mystery if ur down for a documentary
9. Joy, a 2015 movie

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Movie that clearly deserved a sequel…

**Score:** 1256
**Body:** The A-Team(2010)

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. But there is no plan b
  - Fun movie.
2. Hell yes!


It was the perfect example of how to treat retro source material, unlike all the other garbage that was based on 70s-80s tv shows.


Unfortunately, the plan never came together.
3. The tank scene alone is worth it!
  - It was so eye rollingly outrageous I fucking loved it, lol. . . It was all a bit too far fetched but damn was it a fun time
    - It’s was so on brand for an A-Team shenanigan. I love that movie unapologetically.
    - Whaa... you mean flying a tank is not possible?
  - I love Biel's line delivery in that scene, *exasperated* "No, They're trying to fly that tank..."
4. I liked that movie way more than I thought I would, and the scene of them flying the tank was just amazing, grade-A action cheese, and I loved it.
5. I agree. It was a fun action movie. Sharlto Copley as Murdock was especially excellent. 


But I believe it was simply not successful enough to warrant a sequel.

## 2. What are some hidden details that people tend to miss in films?

**Score:** 1094
**Body:** 
I recently read somewhere that in Shutter Island, >! there’s a guard standing right behind Teddy (Leo DiCaprio) and not behind Chuck (Mark Ruffalo), kinda giving us a glimpse of who Teddy really was all along…!< 

What are some interesting details or even clever foreshadowing that we tend to miss until particularly pointed out later during rewatches? Please use spoiler tags wherever necessary. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Leo is always asking for a light because he isn't allowed to have matches.
  - Ruffalo also has trouble handling his firearm when they arrive at the island and must disarm.  No DUSM would fumble around like he did.
2. In Fight Club, when Tyler and the Narrator got on the bus, only the Narrator paid the bus fare. 

But the audience ignored because Tyler not paying the bus fare is in line with his character.
3. A subtle, but meaningful detail in The Truman Show, "Vitamin D" is seen in his medicine, a nod to his artificial world without a real sun.
4. Well, Fight Club constantly tells you what the big plot twist is throughout the movie, and everyone misses it.

I was practically kicking myself on my second viewing seeing all the times they telegraphed it.
5. In Memento, an early scene shows Leonard speaking to Natalie at her house, and she says Dodd beat her up (she has a black eye). They sit down, and as they talk, Leonard is rubbing his hand as if it is bothering him.  Later, we find out why. He is the one who struck Natalie, which he doesn't remember, and it was all part of a plan to set him up
6. In The Departed, Everytime somebody dies there is an X in the scene.
7. In Training Day I didnt notice this at first but there a scene where Alonzo gets the phone call >!before meeting up with the Mexican Gangsters " Make sure that Bathtub is clean " he was definitely planning on killing Jake right away!<
  - Also connected - after Jake beats up the crackheads and saves the girl - she says that her cousins are from hillside trece. That's what gave Alonzo the idea to use those guys to set up Jake. 

People at the time thought it was too much a coincidence that the girl's wallet saved Jake in the bathtub, but it's not a coincidence at all. This is confirmed by Antoine Fuqua in the bluray commentary. Great detail.
8. Dunno how well this is know now, but in Sixth Sense there is always the color red around a supernatural event or person. (kept vague so as to no spoiler a 26 year old movie.)

## 3. What are some of the most intense scenes in films?

**Score:** 853
**Body:** Just read somewhere that Sean Penn requested for a tank of Oxygen to be kept for when he’d finish his shot and decided to deprive himself of breath to get a satisfying take in this scene. Additionally, Clint Eastwood had to bring in two more guys as backup cops, because the original crew in that scene couldn’t control Penn by themselves. 

Of course, he went on to win the Oscar for Best Actor In A Leading Role for Mystic River in 2003. 

What are some of the most intense, well-acted scenes in films that come to mind? Please do share anecdotes, if any. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The police station scene in Manchester by the Sea
2. Jodie Foster when she's in the dark at the end of Silence of the Lambs. Though IIRC they filmed it fully lit as to capture the effects better. And that makes her performance just that much more impressive in my book.
  - Yeah, that scene.  In fact the whole interaction between Clarice and Buffalo Bill was well done.
    - Then Buffalo Bill went to the police academy and became Monk’s boss
3. The car assault in Children of Men
4. Take ur pick what scene u want. Hugh Jackman in Prisoners.
5. The Abyss scene where one of the character has drown to get saved. So intense that everyone misses the camera man wiping the lenses clean.
6. Sicario border scene
  - They build it up for 20 min, “if something is going to happen it will happen at the boarder”. And they don’t disappoint.
7. Saving private Ryan; Knife scène

## 4. What's your favourite Christopher Walken role?

**Score:** 250

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Fatboy Slim music video.
  - Don't be shocked by 

The tone of my voice. 

Check out my new weapon.

Weapon of choice (be careful he loaded)

Walk without rhythm, and it won't attract the worm. 

If you walk without rhythm, you never learn.
2. Joe dirt.
3. Pulp Fiction
4. He’s awesome in Severance.
5. True Romance
6. The Deer Hunter
7. Suicide kings, followed by Blast from the Past
8. The Continental
9. The Dead Zone

## 5. Acting you’ve enjoyed from “non-actors”?

**Score:** 231
**Body:** Not a really a big Chelsea Handler fan, but her acting in the 2012 movie *Fun Size* caught me off guard!

I’ve always seen her as a comedian so it was neat to watch her play \*slight spoiler\* a newly widowed mother who slowly comes to terms with her grief while trying to be present for her children.

As for the movie itself I think it’s cute and fun… I make a point to watch it every Halloween season. (That said I suspect it struggled at the box office partly because of its PG‑13 rating: too adult for kids, but too family‑oriented for adults.)

I’d love to hear other people’s “Oh dang, I didn’t realize \_\_\_\_\_\_ could act!” experiences.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I always like Andre 3000 in Four Brothers
  - Andre 3000 in Semi Pro. “Who the fuck is Bambi?”
  - He was pretty good in Be Cool too .
2. City of God had a bunch of non actors in it, and they all killed it. R Lee Ermey in Full Metal Jacket.
3. Alice Cooper in Wayne's World is so chill.
  - Actually, it's pronounced "mill-e-wah-que" which is Algonquin for "the good land."
4. Roddy Piper in They Live
5. David Bowie in Labyrinth.
6. Idk who I expected would be the best wrestler turned actor, but I can tell you Batista definitely wouldn't have been my first guess.
  - I am always pulling for Dave Bautista. I saw him on Running Wild with Bear Grylls a couple years ago and he talked about his struggles after he physically couldn't wrestle anymore. He was nearly homeless when he got a break in acting and he has worked hard to better his craft. I think he is a genuine person with a great presence onscreen. I wish him nothing but success.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Suggest some great YouTube channels for cinephiles

**Score:** 109
**Body:** Please suggest some great YouTube channels for cinephiles which contains beautifully crafted content related to all kind of cinema, that may be classic, cult classics, neo noire film, even modern films, also discussion with the directors with there movies, movies suggestions, recommendations, all. Want to know some of these youtube channels to acquire more deep knowledge in the world of cinema.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. [Moviewise](https://www.youtube.com/@Moviewise/videos) is a great source. He focuses mainly on what directors do. His vids are detailed, offer a wealth of great examples from multiple countries and generations, and he mixes hard knowledge of his subject with humor and a clear affection for cinema. 

 His channel wouldn't replace a degree from a good film school but it's full of the kind of information a degree like that would bestow. A great channel imo, well worth checking out, and if you sort by popularity all of the vids with over 100k views are worth watching (and all the other ones, too).
2. I'm not a huge fan of the youtube videoessay-industrial complex in general, but two I manage to stomach (if not outright enjoy) are [Accented Cinema](https://www.youtube.com/@AccentedCinema) and [WatchingtheAerial.](https://www.youtube.com/@WatchingtheAerial)   
  
The first focuses primarily on cinema from "Asia" in the broadest sense, but it touches on other areas of the Global South. He takes the time to explain context that might be unfamiliar from people not from the area. [His videos on Chinese cinema are particularly interesting,](https://youtu.be/IUwsTHGY4Ms?si=TbnJ6P7RicxVhtEE) probably because he's from there. Sometimes he likes to make huge sweeping claims about the Nature of the Medium™ that I find a bit silly but I just roll my eyes and go on.  
      
The second is a favorite of mine and talks primarily about cinematography and lenses. It's not so technical that a person who isn't schooled in the science won't understand, but he tries to convey how certain choices contribute to narrative and aesthetics. [His video on Bi Gan is a gem.](https://youtu.be/FpQyVYyLYEA?si=-F66c0mU22P9GItC)
3. I feel like Kogonada deserves a shout out. He was one of the first video essayists that ever popped up on my radar, and in a way it feels like no one ever quite topped him or EveryFrameAPainting.

He's one of the rare essayists that actually let the images create the thesis of the video, rather than having voice over, which I think is kind of the "point" that a lot of video essayists miss.

He's also a film director now! Good for him.
4. Accented Cinema: [https://www.youtube.com/@AccentedCinema](https://www.youtube.com/@AccentedCinema)

He provides an interesting and rare perspective on Asian films (across Asia as a continent, which includes South Asia/Bollywood and Central Asia/Mongolian films), balanced commentary, and is concise. It's worth checking out, if only to discover something new and refreshing. It isn't just arthouse either, but you also have popular and mainstream recommendations from different countries. For instance, he had a recommendation for a Tamil movie I never heard of, but apparently it broke records in India.
5. Like stories of old

Thomas flight

Cinema Nippon

Kolo kino

Bushido blues

House of tabula

Zettai Japan

Screen Crush


Writing extra things so the message doesn't get deleted. Writing extra things so the message doesn't get deleted. Writing extra things so the message doesn't get deleted. Writing extra things so the message doesn't get deleted. Writing extra things so the message doesn't get deleted. Writing extra things so the message doesn't get deleted. Writing extra things so the message doesn't get deleted. Writing extra things so the message doesn't get deleted. Writing extra things so the message doesn't get deleted. Writing extra things so the message doesn't get deleted. Writing extra things so the message doesn't get deleted.
  - Yeah, I quite liked [Like Stories Of Old](https://www.youtube.com/@LikeStoriesofOld) with its meditative style and blend of film and philosophy. Looks good too.
6. I recommend [Studiobinder](https://www.youtube.com/@StudioBinder), [Nerdwriter](https://www.youtube.com/@Nerdwriter1) and [Lessons from the Screenplay](https://www.youtube.com/@LessonsfromtheScreenplay) if you're interested in film analysis from a screenwriting perspective. The latter doesn't post anymore but has an extensive back catalogue.

[Mark Kermode](https://www.youtube.com/kermodeandmayostake) is one of the few remaining reviewers out there offering unbiased and critical takes on new releases. I don't always agree with his taste but his response to films are always well articulated.

[Pop Culture Detective](https://www.youtube.com/@PopCultureDetective) offers fantastic social and ideological criticism of trends in film and television. He's made me aware of so many interesting and, occasionally, troubling trends in filmmaking that I was aware of but never could articulate as clearly as he does.

Lastly, being a bit of a history buff, I'm always curious about the veracity of films based on true stories or actual historical events. [Reel History](https://www.youtube.com/@ReelHistory) and [History Hit](https://www.youtube.com/@HistoryHit) often do expert deep dives by into cinema and television from a historical perspective.
  - >[Mark Kermode](https://www.youtube.com/kermodeandmayostake) is one of the few remaining reviewers out there offering unbiased and critical takes on new releases. I don't always agree with his taste but his response to films are always well articulated.

and brief.

Some people's Youtube reviews are longer than the movie. Kermode sums it up in less than 10 minutes.

  
I'll throw in [BeKindRewind](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCNiolZNLiJplmCCzqk9-czQ)
7. [Cinema Cities](https://youtu.be/blEd5mh1ZX0?si=HwpziTVBCzX4pAle) - she does an outstanding job of analyzing films of the 30’s - 60’s.  Fairly long form videos, and she tends to just post 1 or 2 a month, but they’re all great.
8. Ok it’s in French and I guess most of people here will not be able to follow. And I don’t know if there’s anything like English subtitles or automatic translation on this channel, but "[Cinema et Politique](https://youtube.com/@cinemaetpol?si=kxtL0bt1KqRqar3b)" might be one of the best out there at the moment in the french scene. Each video is excessively documented and goes in lengths about each subject. And the author is not afraid to tackle really tough topics, like the mondo movies, birth of a nation, the passion of christ from Mel Gibson, the working class goes to heaven (and many more Italian movies because Italians were the absolute best in political cinema for at least 2 or 3 decades), stuff like that. 

Lately they’ve released a small documentary about the history of the Locarno film festival and it’s fantastic. Full of stories and information about the tumultuous years before the festival finally got some kind of balance and regularity. 

Anyway, I cannot recommend this channel enough and I would love to know if there’s anything like it in English.

## 2. Burning (2018) Makes Sense If You Admit One Thing: Jong-su Needed Ben to Be The Monster

**Score:** 16
**Body:** TL;DR: Ben isn’t the monster — he’s the mirror. Jong-su kills him not because he’s guilty, but because he reveals everything Jong-su can’t face about himself.

I’ve seen a lot of theories about Burning, and most of them boil down to one idea: Ben is a wealthy serial killer or trafficker (or both), Hae-mi is one of his victims, and Jong-su uncovers the truth as a way to give his empty life meaning. Others argue that Jong-su simply wrote a story, and that most of the film never really happened. These readings are compelling, but honestly? The more I rewatch the film, the more they feel… too Hollywood.

This is a Korean film. Korean films rarely reward literal readings. They dive beneath the surface. They reward emotional truth, projection, and the way people quietly destroy themselves. So here’s the interpretation I’ve come up with:

Hae-mi isn’t dead.
Ben isn’t a killer.
And the real tragedy is that Jong-su builds a murder mystery in his own mind because the truth about himself and his mistakes is too painful to face. 
Let’s begin 

The Cat Named Boyle Is Everything
People obsess over the cat like it’s a Sherlock Holmes clue. But it was more psychological than anything else. Deep down, Hae-mi knows Jong-su. They were childhood friends. She probably picked up on that he’s he’s passive, coasts, doesn’t commit to things, and never reaches out and takes anything in front of him. We see this happen multiple times in the film which I’ll cover later. So what does she do about this? She invents something fragile and dependent — a cat named Boyle — and asks him to take care of it. It’s a test. To see if he’s changed. He fails miserably. It’s true he never sees the cat, but he never acts responsibly to find the cat or call Hae-Mi about it. So he masturbates in her apartment instead of caring for it, and leaves the space messy. Boyle isn’t real because Hae-mi is testing if Jong-su can take care of even an imaginary responsibility — and he can’t.” And that’s the first push toward her leaving. It’s sets up quiet momentum for the entire story.

The Roadside Scene: The Moment He disappoints her again
This is the scene that breaks my heart every time. Hae-mi waits for Jong-su to offer her a ride. She’s literally giving him another chance to step forward, to choose her, to show that he can act. But Jong-su freezes. He shows no sign he will take the lead here. Then Ben pulls up in his immaculate car, confident and effortless, and Hae-mi gets in. Jong-su just fails to show up…again

THE YAWN + THE INSULT: TWO FORMS OF THE SAME DISMISSAL
Once you see it, you can’t unsee it Ben and Jong-su respond to Hae-mi’s vulnerability in almost identical ways — just coated in different personalities. Ben yawns at the dance during the public gathering and doesn’t care how’s its perceived. When she does it at Jong-Su’s house later, he insults her and this upsets her as Ben drives her home. They see her the same almost. Ben just does it with quiet detachment. Jong-su with insecurity and control. But the effect is the same: they both flatten her. They both shrink her. They both reveal they either can’t or won’t go with her into the deeper, emotional, self-actualizing space she craves. Hae-mi is reaching toward something—meaning, expression, transformation, a self she’s never fully accessed. She wants to be seen in that journey.

So Did She Disappear? Yes. But Not the Way People Think.
Hae-mi disappears because she wants to start over. When we first meet her it looks almost like a rehearsal to her starting over, a happy and thriving woman. In reality, her family dismisses he, she has an unstable job, Jong-su doesn’t respect her and this pushed her toward plastic surgery. Ben is detached, and not what she needs. She dreams of vanishing — of evaporating into a horizon where no one knows her past. It’s the best choice she can make right now. The phone call with the breathing is the last chance for jong su to show hae mi that he’s going to step up. And he doesn’t. These repeated failures on Jong su’s part show how powerless someone can feel when they are the bottom of a class system. When there mistakes ripple in their family. When they have passions of their own and never see them through. So how does he expect to see himself committing to a relationship for Hae Mi.
It just goes to show that even if they did get together, it would not be healthy for either party whatsoever.

About That Drawer of Jewelry…
Everyone points to Ben’s bathroom drawer as the proof of his crimes. But it feels more like a performance than evidence — a curated persona he uses to cope with his own emptiness. Or, even more interestingly, something he planted after sensing Jong-su’s paranoia. Ben is observant. He is socially sharper than Jong-su. He reads people almost instantly. I wouldn’t put it past him to “decorate” his bathroom a little after realizing Jong-su is snooping. Not to frame himself as a killer… but to amuse himself. To play with a man too insecure to fight back. He’s bored. And bored people play games.

Ben Always Knew Jong-su Was Following Him — and That’s the Point
One of the most overlooked dynamics in the film is that Ben isn’t oblivious. He clocked Jong-su’s surveillance almost immediately. The quiet car-following scenes? Ben absolutely knew. The gym moment where he spots Jong-su staring at him? He knew. The dinner where he glances at him with that faint, amused smile? He knew. And why didn’t he confront him or seem afraid? Because Jong-su has never once shown himself to be someone who takes action. Ben knows this. He’s watched Jong-su fail to commit to anything: to ambition, to Hae-mi, to self-respect. Ben feels untouchably powerful because:

1)He didn’t kill Hae-mi, so there is no secret to expose.
2)He’s certain Jong-su will never do anything, because passivity is Jong-su’s entire identity.


Jong su confronting Ben about Hae Mi’s whereabouts.
This scene is big and so misread. Jong-su keeps demanding answers from Ben like Ben is the gatekeeper to Hae-mi’s fate. And Ben just… steps back from it.He basically says:
“Why are you asking me?
You’re the one who knew her.
This is your mess, not mine.”
He refuses to enter the fantasy Jong-su is building.
It’s the first moment where we see the gap between them: Ben lives in reality despite his narcissism. Jong-su lives in projection to escape himself.

As a result, Jong-su isn’t Investigating a Murder — He Invents One
This is the part of the film that feels the most tragic. Jong-su can’t face the real story:
He judged the girl who loved him.
He never claimed her.
He never protected her.
He let her walk away.
He never took responsibility for anything in his life.

So instead of admitting this, he creates a narrative where:
she was taken
she was killed
Ben is a villain
he is a hero
and the story is larger than his own regret. The murder mystery is a coping mechanism. A fantasy where he matters, where his passivity becomes righteousness, where Ben becomes the face of all the insecurity and shame he refuses to deal with. To deal with being the at bottom of the social ladder and the people who failed him, like his father.

Now the Ending…
When Jong-su kills Ben, Ben didn’t expect this because of who Jong-Su was this entire film. Even we didn’t expect it. It’s the first time Ben’s psychological structure was dismantled by a man he saw as beneath him. So when he has no choice but to hug him, to get SOME control back, it’s humiliating to his social self and he weeps. This gives the makeup scene an entirely different meaning: it never happened. It was just one of Jong-Su’s stories that he wrote or believed to be true to further demonize Ben. Both men succumbed to the battle of class and the self. Jong-Su believed he was killing the man he projected his failures onto, the rival he exaggerated in his mind, the symbol of everything he envied and couldn’t face, but he really couldn’t face the truth. He used Ben as ploy to fight the girl he lost his chance at having.

In the end, Jong-su doesn’t kill a murderer. He kills the man who exposed what he couldn’t accept about himself, no matter how twisted and cruel that way of exposing him was.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Love this movie. I don’t agree with your theory but I think it highlights the film’s beautiful ambiguity. I read the short story it was based on and considering that, I think the more basic reading of the underlying plot is true.
  - Hi! I’ve been meaning to the read the short story to see what details were changed or cut for the movie. Thank for reading. What was your theories for the film overall?
2. This is an amazing reading. It's been a while since I saw the film, but it really stayed with me after watching (and I actually watched it again right after), so I think I remember most of it. Still, your thoughts make me want to go watch it again.


Whatever reading one may get out of it, it's undeniably a very tragic story. I haven't read the original story, but I did read Tokio Blues, and if that story's protagonist is anything like the one from Barn Burning, then it seems that Murakami's stories are able to thoroughly describe a very particular, yet unfortunately very widespread kind of man: one who, in his constant shielding of himself, in his refusal to take responsibility and show commitment for anything, ends up not only shutting himself off, but also hurting anyone who attempts to connect with them. In Burning, that someone is Hae-mi, but it could have been any other girl who made the mistake of opening herself up to someone like either Ben (who doesn't care about her) or Jong-su (who is unwilling to reciprocate).
  - Yes! I definitely agree this movie explores the social angles of men and their deepest selves in such a nuanced and fresh way, that can’t be denied. I definitely need to look into Haruki’s works and give them a read. The premise for Norwegian Wood is interesting. I think one thing about these mystery thriller movies is we can get so caught in the meaning and logic behind every plot beat, but sometimes it’s not about that at all, just what is felt and how that can be debated or argued. A lot of what we feel and our personal experience can fuel these interpretations and that’s the beauty of films like this. Burning is undoubtedly a tragic story from 3 point of views (or maybe one, if Jong-Su is narrating everything) and it’s hard to justify what their point of views of are telling us, but it most certainly be felt.
    - I liked Norwegian Wood but it can definitely be tiring or even offensive in its depiction of women. I guess I liked it partly because I chose to read those elements as more indicative of the narrator's mind space rather than as the author's actual views. And, interpreting it that way, the links between it and Burning (and presumably Barn Burning) are clear.


Have you seen Memories of Murder? I think it relates a lot to what you said about thrillers, although the story and message are very different from Burning (and also the thriller/mystery elements are a lot more prevalent).
3. I shared some thoughts on it in a couple of replies to a post about it on this sub which went a little under the radar considering the thoughtfulness of the post, here:

https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/comments/1mi9k7i/existence_meaning_and_ambiguity_an_analysis_and/

## 3. Day For Night: a wonderful introduction into the filmography of Francois Truffaut

**Score:** 11
**Body:** I've been putting off the film for a while now, since I felt like movies about movies can sometimes miss the mark when it comes to being repetitive or maybe superficial despite the sincerity of the filmmaker. Boy was I happily mistaken; day for night presents an expansive look into the detailed works that goes into making a film, mixing both the technical professionalism and the charming drama that a good Nouvelle Vague film does well. I, Especially, felt the company of the director Ferrand to be very pleseant in his calm, easy going demeanor met with his know how and ardour, along with his focus on his art while everything around him plunge into chaos. I can  go on and on about how every actor and actress bring add in their charm and into the set of the film that was being made.

 The film being a love letter to filmmaking; it is overflowing with film magic in both the fake film and the actual film, seeing the combination of built in sets with the established cameras and the way actors go about their roles is absolutely magical. Additionally Truffaut films the filmmaking process with such a passion and awareness of the magic he creates the way his character guides his the film crew, and with those montages.

A film like this is the reason why I love films.

## 4. The Secret Agent: cinema as a means of preserving memory

**Score:** 7
**Body:** I've watched The Secret Agent about a week ago, which is the new film from Kleber Mendonça Filho (Neighbouring Sounds, Aquarius, Bacurau). It premiered at the main competition in Cannes earlier this year, and Cannes bent its rules to give the film two awards: best director and best actor for its protagonist, Wagner Moura. The film was also chosen as the brazilian submission for the Oscar for best foreign film, and many critics are pointing out there's high chances for Wagner Moura to also be nominated for best actor.

I was simply enchanted by this film in a way I haven't felt in a long time. I feel it left me energized and impacted in a way that almost reinvigorated my taste for cinema.

The film takes place in the 1970s, is set in the city of Recife, and tells the story of a university professor who was dismissed from his position and is being pursued by assassins linked to members of the brazilian military dictatorship. He tries to remain on the down low while waiting for false documents to be ready so he can flee Brazil with his young son. The narrative is non-linear, showing events that occurred before the conflict in the film and also scenes in the present day, where some university students are researching the protagonist's story through journalistic articles and listening to cassette tapes with recordings of his conversations.

I can perhaps summarize the film's strength in its very harmonious way of dealing with serious issues (both the insidious aspect of interference by the military dictatorship in Brazil and the extent to which these conflicts disrupted the personal and family lives of many people), while using this as a backdrop for a story that borders on a very entertaining pulpy action thriller. I've seen some people refer to it as "Once Upon a Time in Recife," and now I understand why.

Some people disliked the ending because they feel that expectations were created that were not met in the ending, by not showing in detail what should have been the climax, >!in which the protagonist is killed. And, worse, the protagonist's son, an adult also played by Wagner Moura, claims to have very little memory of his father.!<

For me, this ending is great precisely because it synthesizes the film's message that the greatest tragedy left as a legacy of the dictatorship was the fact that many people affected by personal loss did not have the right to keep their memories of that period alive. Many of those who died during this period were kidnapped and their bodies were buried as indigents. It literally took decades for many of these people to receive their death certificates, and even then, many took even longer for those certificates to indicate that their deaths were violent and perpetrated by the military dictatorship. Many people who suffered losses during this period did not have the chance to properly grieve.

Combining this with the incredibly rich detail present in the film's set design, especially the use of a traditional movie theater as the setting for several key scenes, corroborates a proposition that, once I realized it, maximized my experience of the film: this is a fictional film that understands that the role of cinema, even fictional cinema, is to help ensure that the memory of a time, a place, events, and people does not disappear.

This is an attitude present in several of the filmmaker's films. To quote a review from Letterboxd written by the Brazilian film critic Arthur Tuoto, which I really enjoyed, talking about the film's ending:

"it highlights what are perhaps the great universal and constant motivations for Kleber: cinema and people, each trying to save the other from oblivion."

I liked how it works as a thriller, but also as a sort of anti-thriller, for example with the ending. Also with the slightly languid pace and the attention to just life itself, like how the community of outcasts from the regime is formed in the apartment complex. 

Another thing I really liked and you didn't mention is how absurd it is. Present from the very first scene, and then it's ramped up in another scene that I don't want to spoil, but despite it being absurd, it always feels grounded within the reality of the world itself.

## 5. The political themes in Scarface

**Score:** 6
**Body:** Scarface, as you know, has a theme of the American dream. As the film starts in the 1980 Mariel boatlift. Tony Montana (Al Pacino) is a victim of communism under Castro's dictatorship. The Mariel Boatlift was a time when Cuban immigrants faced discrimination and xenophobia. Tony's first victim is a communist named Emilio Rebenga. It was a politically motivated kill so that Tony and Manny Ribera can get their green cards, and also Frank Lopez wanted them to kill Rebenga because Frank's brother was killed by Rebenga. 

There's also Tony boasting about how great it would've been had he come to America years prior, believing he would've been a millionaire. He refers to Miami as a "great big pussy just waiting to get fucked" After Manny is slapped by a woman for making a snake tongue gesture at her, Tony tells Manny how to achieve the American dream. 

"In this country, you got to make the money first. Then when you get the money, you get the power. Then when you get the power, then you get the woman." 

In the restaurant scene, Tony complains about how empty and hollow his life has become. 

When Alejandro Sosa calls Tony down to Bolivia for a meeting about his tax evasion arrest, he introduces him to several influential figures, like a sugar corporation chairman, a military general, and an American official from Washington. Sosa says that he has some "friends in Washington" that could help Tony. The implication is that the drug trade between the U.S. and Latin-America are being kept alive by organized sources by those high up in the government. Director Sidney Lumet left the development of Scarface because he wanted the film to be about that very subject. The political themes are still there even under Brian De Palma's direction.
